one_line_takeaway: A retraining-free LLM weights-watermark that solves a linear constraint against a Transformer QK^T invariant so ownership marks survive functional-equivalence transforms, achieving 100% clean detection with near-zero accuracy loss but limited tolerance to heavy fine-tuning and to more than ~8 colluding users.
key_finding: The scheme achieves 100% watermark detection in single- and multi-user modes on all three models under clean conditions with negligible fidelity loss (Llama3-8B mean over 10 benchmarks: 65.56 vanilla vs 65.58 watermarked). It is robust to functional-equivalence transforms by construction (Table 1 claims it resists all of FT/pruning/quantization/permutation/scaling/reversible/collusion, versus EmMark and Fernandez which each fail several). Under attacks: ~100% detection at pruning ratio 0.1 (beyond 0.3 model accuracy collapses anyway); 100% detection under 4/8-bit quantization except Gemma-2B 4-bit multi-user which drops to 0.84; fine-tuning up to 50k samples largely preserved detection while heavy 100k fine-tuning degrades it (Table 4). Analytically, with l=50, beta=0.2, rho=30 and 80% top-20% ranking, extraction success = 99.96% and false-attribution to a wrong user at 10^5 users = 0.007%.
core_contribution: The paper (Lenovo Research) proposes an invariant-based, parameter-space ('weights') watermarking scheme for Transformer LLMs that embeds an owner/user watermark directly into the embedding-layer weight matrix WITHOUT any retraining or fine-tuning. Its central technical idea is to bind the watermark to a structural invariant of the Transformer: it computes an invariant matrix Am from QK^T over selected embedding rows (Algorithm 1), then solves a linear system Am(Mw)^T = 0 to derive watermark values that satisfy this constraint, so the watermark survives functional-equivalence transforms (permutation/scaling/invertible-matrix attacks) that leave model behavior unchanged. Each user gets a unique permutation-matrix key, enabling traceability. A multi-user extension adds orthogonal-transform 'noise' at non-watermark positions to mask watermark locations and resist collusion. The authors frame it as the first retraining-free weights-watermark for edge/on-device LLMs. (Note: prior methods DeepSigns, DeepMarks, Uchida, EmMark, Fernandez are cited as RELATED WORK/baselines, not this paper's contribution.)
threat_model: Consistent with Feng et al. 2024 and Zhang & Koushanfar (EmMark) 2024: the attacker (a malicious user of an on-device/edge-deployed LLM) has FULL white-box access to the watermarked model parameters and partial knowledge of the watermark embedding algorithm, but does NOT have the original (unwatermarked) weights and does NOT know the random permutation keys used to select watermark positions. Goal is IP theft / unauthorized redistribution. Two attack families are considered: (1) removal attacks - fine-tuning, pruning, quantization, and transformation attacks (permutation, scaling, reversible/invertible-matrix) that exploit functional-equivalence; plus collusion attacks (multiple users averaging/copy-pasting their copies to erase the mark); (2) ambiguity/confusion attacks where an adversary embeds their own watermark or reverse-computes a fake 'original' to claim ownership.
limitations: Fine-tuning robustness degrades sharply with data size - at 100k fine-tuning samples single-user detection falls to 40-58% at beta=1% for Gemma-2B and Llama3-8B (Table 4), so the 'robust to fine-tuning' claim is scale-limited. Collusion tolerance is small: only up to ~8 colluding users detectable for Llama3-8B (t=5, scale_wm=1000), and varies with parameters. Gemma-2B (lower-capacity) needs relaxed thresholds (rho=28) giving 98.77% success but 0.2% wrong-user rate. The scheme depends on the owner retaining the original unwatermarked weights for cosine-similarity permutation recovery and for ambiguity-attack resolution. Watermark is embedded only in the single embedding layer (authors note future work to spread across layers). The strong Table 1 superiority claim over EmMark/Fernandez is asserted qualitatively; no head-to-head robustness numbers against those baselines are reported (only an efficiency comparison to Fernandez). Pruning 'robustness' partly rests on the argument that higher pruning destroys the model anyway rather than on the watermark surviving.

datasets_used: OpenCompass evaluation suite (10 tasks) for fidelity: CEval, MMLU, WiC, WSC, CoPA, CB, BoolQ, PiQA, MultiRC, HellaSwag
Fine-tuning attack datasets: Blossom-Math-ZH, Alpaca-ZH, MS-Bench (10k/50k/100k subsets)
quantitative_results: Clean watermark detection rate: 100% for single- and multi-user on all three models (Table 2)
Llama3-8B fidelity mean over 10 benchmarks: 65.56 (vanilla) vs 65.58 (watermarked, t=5..20) (Table 3)
Pruning: ~100% detection at ratio 0.1; accuracy collapses beyond ratio 0.3
Quantization: 100% detection at 4-bit and 8-bit, except Gemma-2B 4-bit multi-user = 0.84 (Figure 6)
Analytical extraction success 99.96% at l=50, beta=0.2, rho=30 (80% top-20%); wrong-user rate 0.007% at 10^5 users
Gemma-2B (70% top-20%): 98.77% success at rho=28, 0.2% wrong-user rate
Collusion: up to 8 colluding users detectable for Llama3-8B at t=5, scale_wm=1000
Fixed hyperparameters: l=50, t in {5,10,15,20}, beta 0.01-0.3, rho 30-50; optimal (t=10, wm_num=50, scale_wm=1000)
baselines_compared: EmMark (Zhang & Koushanfar 2024) - robustness comparison Table 1 (fails pruning, permutation, scaling, reversible, collusion per authors)
Fernandez et al. 2024 (functional invariants to watermark large transformers) - Table 1 robustness comparison and an efficiency comparison on Llama3-8B
Cited prior-art (not directly benchmarked): Uchida et al. 2017, DeepSigns, DeepMarks
